question: Define a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) and explain its significance in the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: A DFA is formally defined as a 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) where Q is a finite set of states, Σ is a finite input alphabet, δ: Q × Σ → Q is the transition function, q0 is the start state, and F ⊆ Q is the set of accept states.
It is a simple, abstract model of a computing machine that reads an input string one symbol at a time and changes its state deterministically based on the current state and the input symbol.

DFAs are significant because they recognize precisely the class of regular languages, providing a foundational model for understanding what can be computed with limited memory and for the study of formal languages and grammars.

question: Explain the core argument of the Church-Turing thesis and why it is central to the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: The Church-Turing thesis is a hypothesis stating that any function that is effectively computable by an algorithm is computable by a Turing machine.
It equates the informal notion of an 'algorithm' or 'effective procedure' with the formal, mathematical model of a Turing machine.

This thesis is central because it establishes the Turing machine as a universal model of computation, providing a foundation for defining what is computable and what is not, and for studying the limits of computation through decidability and complexity theory.

question: What is the main difference between the set of languages accepted by deterministic finite automata (DFAs) and those accepted by nondeterministic finite automata (NFAs)?
expectedPoints: There is no difference in the set of languages they accept.
Both DFAs and NFAs recognize exactly the same class of languages: the regular languages.
This is proven by a construction algorithm that shows any NFA can be converted into an equivalent DFA (though possibly with an exponential increase in the number of states).
